text: Code

How to administer

Read all three one-line descriptions in a randomised order (rotate first position across interviews to reduce order bias — note the order in notes).
Ask: “Which of these sounds most like something your firm needs — and why?”
Then ask: “Is anything here overpromised or not credible?”
Record the chosen framing in positioning_preference and the reasoning verbatim in notes.
Do not editorialise or defend a framing — you are measuring reaction, not selling.


Bias control. Never reveal which framing is “ours”. If the respondent asks, say all three are under consideration. Leading here corrupts the single most important D2 signal — reflect any leading in a higher bias_risk score.

Option 1 — CRM Platform
Short description (read aloud): > “A system of record for your clients and pipeline — contacts, deals, and activity in one place, so > nothing falls through the cracks.”

What we are testing: whether “CRM” is instantly understood but perceived as generic / commoditised.
Interpretation: high comprehension + low excitement = clear but generic. If chosen mainly because it’s familiar (not because it’s compelling), that supports H-POS-CRM-01. Genuine enthusiasm would refute it.
Informs claim: H-POS-CRM-01 — “CRM positioning may be clear but too generic.”

Option 2 — Revenue Operations Platform
Short description (read aloud): > “A platform that connects your commercial workflow end-to-end — pipeline, delivery, and the handoffs > between them — so you can see and improve how revenue actually gets made.”

What we are testing: whether a RevOps framing expresses business value better than “CRM” without losing clarity.
Interpretation: preference for this framing because it maps to a felt operational problem supports H-POS-REVOPS-01. Confusion about what it means (needs explaining) is a caution — note it. Watch for respondents who like the words but can’t say what it does.
Informs claim: H-POS-REVOPS-01 — “Revenue Operations Platform positioning may better express business value.”

Option 3 — AI Readiness Platform
Short description (read aloud): > “A platform that gets your client and commercial data structured and clean, so your firm is ready to > use AI to work faster and smarter.”

What we are testing: whether the AI framing is differentiated and exciting or overpromising and not credible for this buyer today.
Interpretation: excitement + belief = differentiated (note it). Excitement + scepticism (“sounds like hype”, “we’re nowhere near that”) supports H-POS-AI-01 (differentiated but may overpromise). Reasoning about their own data readiness also informs H-AI-READY-01/02 — capture it.
Informs claim: H-POS-AI-01 — “AI Readiness Platform positioning may be differentiated but could overpromise.” (Secondary: H-AI-READY-01, H-AI-READY-02.)

Recording the result








Observation
positioning_preference
Also note in notes




Clear single preference
CRM / RevOps / AI Readiness
the reason, verbatim


Likes two roughly equally
the stronger one
the trade-off they described


No clear preference / confused
unclear
what confused them



Then, when creating evidence records (templates/evidence_capture_template.md), write one record per positioning claim the answer bears on, each with supports / refutes / mixed / neutral and a one-line evidence_summary.

What this test cannot do (yet)

It is comprehension + stated preference, not behaviour. A stated preference is weaker than a won deal. Treat results as grade-C signal at best until corroborated across many interviews.
It does not price the offering — willingness-to-pay is probed separately (script Q11.3).
Do not conclude a positioning from the sprint. The gate for D2 is grade B across converging message tests; a handful of interviews cannot clear it.
Code

0. Interviewer setup (before the call)

Assign interview_id (e.g. INT-0001), record date (YYYY-MM-DD).
Confirm the segment: company_segment = Audit or Professional Services.
Primary decision under test this sprint: D1 (Market Entry) and D2 (Positioning).

Open neutrally: “We’re researching how firms like yours run commercial and client operations. There are no right answers — we’re trying to learn, not sell. May I take notes?”

1. Respondent context
Purpose: establish who is speaking and how much weight their view carries (feeds bias_risk and evidence_quality_score).
Q1.1 — “Tell me about your firm and your role in it.” - Why: sizing and authority; a partner’s budget view differs from an analyst’s. - → context · — · company_size, respondent_role · capture band + role verbatim.
Q1.2 — “How many people touch client acquisition or delivery operations?” - Why: scale of the workflow problem. - → D1 · H-SEG-PROF-01 · notes · qualitative.

2. Current commercial process
Purpose: understand how they actually win and deliver work today — the substrate a CRM would organise.
Q2.1 — “Walk me through how a new client goes from first contact to signed engagement.” - Why: reveals pipeline structure (or its absence) and hand-off pain. - → D1 · H-SEG-PROF-01 · notes, contributes to workflow_maturity.
Q2.2 — “Where in that process do things most often slip or get lost?” - Why: locates the acute pain without naming it for them. - → D1 · H-SEG-AUDIT-01, H-SEG-PROF-01 · notes, contributes to pain_score.

3. Current tooling
Purpose: what they use now, and how mature it is.
Q3.1 — “What tools do you use to track clients, pipeline, and work in progress?” - Why: baseline; distinguishes spreadsheet-shops from CRM-shops. - → D1/D2 · H-POS-CRM-01, H-AI-READY-01 · current_tooling (free text) → informs crm_maturity.
Q3.2 — “What do those tools do well, and where do they fall short?” - Why: separates a tooling-gap from a discipline-gap. - → D1 · H-SEG-PROF-01 · notes.

4. Pain points
Purpose: the core of D1 — is there acute, specific, felt pain?
Q4.1 — “What’s the single most frustrating part of running your commercial workflow?” - Why: the headline pain, in their words. - → D1 · H-SEG-AUDIT-01 (audit), H-SEG-PROF-01 (prof. services) · notes → pain_score.
Q4.2 — “How often does that happen, and who does it affect?” - Why: frequency and blast radius separate a nuisance from a priority. - → D1 · H-SEG-AUDIT-01 · contributes to pain_score.

5. Cost of pain
Purpose: convert pain into money/time — the bridge from pain to budget and urgency.
Q5.1 — “If you had to estimate, what does that problem cost you — in hours, missed work, or rework?” - Why: quantifies pain; a costed problem is a fundable one. - → D1 · H-SEG-AUDIT-01, H-SEG-AUDIT-02 · notes → raises pain_score and budget_signal.
Q5.2 — “Has anyone tried to fix it before? What happened?” - Why: prior spend signals real budget and reveals switching costs. - → D1 · H-SEG-AUDIT-02 · notes → budget_signal.

6. CRM / workflow maturity
Purpose: score how defined and adopted their processes and CRM are (feeds D2 AI-readiness thesis).
Q6.1 — “How standardised are your commercial workflows — same every time, or person-dependent?” - Why: defined workflows are what a CRM (and later AI) can act on. - → D2 · H-AI-READY-02 · workflow_maturity · score 0–100.
Q6.2 — “If you have a CRM today, how much of the team actually lives in it?” - Why: adoption, not ownership, is the real maturity signal. - → D2 · H-POS-CRM-01, H-AI-READY-02 · crm_maturity · score 0–100.

7. Analytics / AI readiness
Purpose: test the AI-readiness claims — do they have structured data, and would a CRM improve it?
Q7.1 — “How do you currently use your data to make commercial decisions?” - Why: existing analytics practice predicts ability to operationalise AI. - → D2 · H-AI-READY-01 · ai_readiness_maturity · score 0–100.
Q7.2 — “Is your client and pipeline data structured and in one place, or scattered?” - Why: directly tests H-AI-READY-01 (customers may lack structured data). - → D2 · H-AI-READY-01 · notes → ai_readiness_maturity.
Q7.3 — “If your CRM were well-adopted, do you think it would make you more ‘AI-ready’? Why?” - Why: tests H-AI-READY-02 (CRM implementation may improve AI readiness). Probe reasoning, not a yes/no. - → D2 · H-AI-READY-02 · notes.

8. Budget signal
Purpose: is there money and a buyer? (D1 · H-SEG-AUDIT-02.)
Q8.1 — “Who would own the budget for a tool like this, and is there budget this year?” - Why: identifies buyer + budget line; distinguishes interest from intent. - → D1 · H-SEG-AUDIT-02 · budget_signal · score 0–100.

9. Urgency signal
Purpose: is there a compelling reason to act now? (D1.)
Q9.1 — “Is this a ‘this quarter’ problem or a ‘someday’ problem? What makes it urgent, or not?” - Why: urgency drives the deal; absence of it predicts stall. - → D1 · H-SEG-AUDIT-01 · urgency_signal · score 0–100.

10. Sales cycle & decision complexity
Purpose: how hard/long is it to buy? (D1 sequencing, D3 gate.)
Q10.1 — “If you decided to adopt something, how long would it take and who would need to sign off?” - Why: short cycles and few stakeholders make a better first segment. - → D1/D3 · H-GTM-GATE-01 · sales_cycle_signal · score 0–100 (higher = shorter/simpler).

11. Positioning test
Purpose: test how the three framings land. Use the full script in templates/positioning_message_test.md; record the outcome here.
Q11.1 — Read the three one-line descriptions (CRM / Revenue Operations / AI Readiness) and ask: “Which of these sounds most like something your firm needs — and why?” - Why: tests the D2 positioning claims against a real buyer. - → D2 · H-POS-CRM-01, H-POS-REVOPS-01, H-POS-AI-01 · positioning_preference (CRM / RevOps / AI Readiness / unclear).
Q11.2 — “Is there anything in those that sounds overpromised or not credible?” - Why: tests H-POS-AI-01 (AI framing may overpromise). - → D2 · H-POS-AI-01 · notes.
Q11.3 (optional WTP probe) — “If one of those solved your headline problem, what would that be worth per month to your firm?” - Why: light willingness-to-pay read; keep it directional, do not anchor. - → D1/D3 · H-SEG-AUDIT-02 · willingness_to_pay_signal · score 0–100.

12. Closing question
Q12.1 — “If you had a magic wand for your commercial operations, what’s the first thing you’d fix?” - Why: an unprompted priority; often the truest signal of the day. - → D1/D2 · H-SEG-AUDIT-01, H-SEG-PROF-01 · notes.

13. Permission to follow up
Q13.1 — “May we come back to you as this develops, and would you be open to trying an early version?” - Why: a soft commitment is itself a signal; also enables the pilot that could reach grade A. - → D3 · H-GTM-GATE-01 · notes.

After the interview (interviewer, same day)

Fill every covered field in one row of data/raw/discovery_interviews.csv.
Score pain_score, budget_signal, urgency_signal, crm_maturity, workflow_maturity, ai_readiness_maturity, willingness_to_pay_signal, sales_cycle_signal, evidence_quality_score, bias_risk using templates/discovery_interview_scoring_guide.md (all 0–100).
Set decision_linked, claim_linked, and supports_or_refutes (supports / refutes / mixed / neutral) for the primary claim the interview bore on. (Secondary claims are captured when you create evidence records.)
Map the interview into data/processed/discovery_evidence.csv — one record per claim — using templates/evidence_capture_template.md.
Code

Context
We are converting a commercial-strategy proposal into a reproducible, collaborative, testable research project — not a slide deck or a static document site. The architecture must let strategy be questioned, computed, tested, and revised as evidence accumulates, and must keep unvalidated numbers from being mistaken for findings.

Decision
Adopt the following stack and structure:

Quarto (website project) — one artifact renders the working paper, dashboards, frameworks, CIS, and decision records together; prose and executable analysis live side by side and stay in sync.
Python + Jupyter execution for .qmd — analysis is code, re-run on every render; results are reproducible rather than pasted.
Pandas + Plotly — tabular data handling and interactive charts with a light dependency set.
A src/ package as the single source of computational truth — scoring, validation, evidence, and ontology logic implemented once and imported by dashboards, notebooks, and tests. No re-implementation of scoring in analysis pages.
YAML specifications (cis/, frameworks/) — ontology, object model, lifecycle, and framework weights are data, editable and auditable independently of code.
Synthetic sample data (data/sample/) — exercises the machinery and renders the site without claiming any real market fact; raw/processed/external zones are reserved for real data later.
Modular scoring functions with dictionary weights — transparent, tunable, and individually testable; every score is a pure function returning [0, 100].
Pytest — scoring ranges, missing-value safety, validation, evidence aggregation, and data integrity are all under automated test; a red suite is a hard stop.
Git-friendly layout — small text files, clear boundaries, append-only decision records.

Consequences
Positive

Any number can be traced to the code, data, and framework that produced it, and re-run.
Contributors change behaviour by editing weights/data, not by rewriting analysis prose.
Tests catch regressions in the load-bearing logic before results are trusted.
The same repository serves reviewers, analysts, and architects.

Negative / costs

Contributors need Python + Quarto installed; a pure-Markdown contributor cannot touch the analysis.
Keeping the scoring weight dicts in src/ in sync with the framework YAML is a manual discipline at v0.1 (mitigated by utils.weights_from_framework and tests).

Alternatives considered

A static docs site (MkDocs/plain Markdown). Rejected: no executable analysis, no reproducibility, and it would invite pasted, un-checkable numbers.
A notebook-only project. Rejected: notebooks are hard to review and diff, and encourage logic duplicated across cells rather than a tested shared core.
A BI dashboard tool. Rejected at v0.1: heavier, less version-controllable, and it separates the reasoning (prose) from the computation.

title: Commercial Intelligence Specification (CIS)
text: Version 0.1.0 Status · Draft Normative · governs process

title: Commercial Intelligence Specification (CIS)
text:
1. Introduction
Commercial strategy is normally recorded as prose and slides: assertions about markets, customers, positioning, and pricing, chosen partly to persuade. Such artifacts are difficult to test, difficult to revise coherently, and give no account of why one recommendation was preferred over another.
The Commercial Intelligence Specification (CIS) defines an alternative: commercial strategy expressed as a computational decision system. Under CIS, strategic claims are structured objects with identity and provenance; recommendations are outputs of transparent, versioned computation over evidence; and the entire chain from question to monitored outcome is reproducible.
CIS is deliberately modest at v0.1. It specifies structure and discipline, not advanced modelling. Sophisticated models can be added later without changing the governance contract.

title: Commercial Intelligence Specification (CIS)
text:
2. Purpose
The purpose of CIS is to ensure that every commercial recommendation produced in this project is:

Traceable — it can name the question, hypothesis, and evidence it derives from.
Reproducible — its numbers are produced by code and data anyone can re-run.
Falsifiable — it states what would prove it wrong.
Revisable — it updates coherently as evidence changes, without silent rewriting of history.
Honest about confidence — it distinguishes hypothesis, weak evidence, and validated fact.

title: Commercial Intelligence Specification (CIS)
text:
3. Vision
A commercial strategy that behaves like a well-run experiment programme rather than a fixed argument. Decisions are versioned. Evidence accumulates. Confidence is measured, not asserted. New information moves recommendations in a documented way. The organisation can always answer: what do we believe, how strongly, and why?

title: Commercial Intelligence Specification (CIS)
text:
4. Guiding principles

Claims before conclusions. Nothing is concluded until it has been stated as a falsifiable claim and tested.
Evidence over eloquence. A well-argued assertion with no evidence outranks nothing.
Transparency of computation. Every score is produced by inspectable code with named weights.
Separation of hypothesis and fact. The proposal PDF is Hypothesis v0.1; it is never silently promoted to evidence.
Weights are data, not code. Tunable parameters live in YAML/config so they can be changed and audited independently of logic.
Reproducibility is non-negotiable. If a result cannot be re-run, it does not exist.
Confidence is explicit. Every recommendation carries a confidence grade and its basis.
History is append-only. Decisions and evidence are superseded, not deleted.
Simplicity first. Prefer the simplest model that answers the question; defer complexity until evidence demands it.

title: Commercial Intelligence Specification (CIS)
text:
5. Scope
In scope (v0.1): market prioritisation, CRM positioning, customer/segment fit, AI readiness assessment, evidence tracking, decision provenance, and the testing standard that guards them.
Out of scope (v0.1): live CRM data integration, production ML pipelines, automated data collection, pricing optimisation at scale, and organisation-wide rollout tooling. These are named in Future Work and must not be faked with unvalidated numbers.

title: Commercial Intelligence Specification (CIS)
text:
6. Core philosophy
CIS treats a commercial strategy as a decision system: a directed process that transforms questions into monitored recommendations through explicit, inspectable stages. The unit of work is not “the strategy” but “the decision” — a single question carried through the lifecycle.
Strategy, in this view, is the accumulated set of decisions plus their evidence and confidence. It is never finished; it is the current best state of a continuing enquiry.

title: Commercial Intelligence Specification (CIS)
text:
7. Core ontology
The ontology defines the objects the system reasons about. It is specified normatively in cis/ontology.yml. Summary:







Object
Meaning




Question
A commercial decision to be made, phrased so it can be answered.


Hypothesis
A falsifiable, provisional answer to a Question.


Operational Definition
How a hypothesis’s terms are measured (what counts, in what units).


Evidence
A dated, sourced observation that supports, refutes, or qualifies a claim.


Framework
A named, weighted scoring scheme that turns inputs into a score.


Model
A computation (framework, statistical model, or rule set) producing an output.


Recommendation
A proposed action derived from models + evidence, with confidence.


Decision
A recorded choice to act (or not), with its provenance and owner.


Metric
A quantity monitored after a decision to test whether it held.



Relationships (see ontology.yml for cardinality): a Question has many Hypotheses; a Hypothesis has an Operational Definition and is supported/refuted by Evidence; Frameworks and Models consume Evidence and produce scores; a Recommendation cites Models and Evidence; a Decision adopts a Recommendation and is monitored by Metrics.

title: Commercial Intelligence Specification (CIS)
text:
8. Object model
The object model in cis/object-model.yml gives each object its fields, types, identity rule, and required provenance. Normative requirements:

Every object has a stable id and a version.
Every object records created and status (draft → active → superseded/retired).
Objects that assert something about the world (Evidence, Recommendation, Decision) MUST carry provenance: who/what produced them, from which sources, when.
Scores are stored with the framework_id and framework_version that produced them, so a score is never orphaned from its method.

title: Commercial Intelligence Specification (CIS)
text:
9. Decision lifecycle
The canonical lifecycle, specified in cis/decision-lifecycle.yml. Every recommendation must be able to trace its provenance through these eleven stages in order:


01Question


→02Hypothesis


→03Operational Definition


→04Evidence Collection


→05Analysis


→06Model


→07Validation


→08Recommendation


→09Implementation


→10Monitoring


→11Learning ⟲








Mandatory. A stage MUST NOT be skipped in the recorded provenance. A Recommendation whose provenance lacks Evidence Collection and Validation is invalid under CIS.




Each artifact in the repository SHOULD declare the lifecycle stage it advances.
Learning is not a terminus: it revises questions and hypotheses, re-entering the loop at stage

This feedback is what makes the strategy “living”.

title: Commercial Intelligence Specification (CIS)
text:
10. Evidence model
Evidence is the currency of the system. Specified operationally in src/evidence.py and the evidence_register.csv schema.

Each Evidence item links to a claim_id and states supports_claim ∈ {yes, no, mixed}.
Each item has a quality_score ∈ [0, 1] (source rigour, recency, directness) and a bias_risk ∈ {low, medium, high}.
Evidence weight = quality_score × bias_adjustment, where bias adjustment penalises higher bias risk. Refuting evidence carries the same weight machinery as supporting evidence.
Evidence strength for a claim is a classification (none / weak / moderate / strong) derived from the aggregate weight of non-conflicting evidence.
A proposal assertion enters as Evidence only with source_type = proposal and is capped in quality; it can raise a hypothesis, not validate one.

title: Commercial Intelligence Specification (CIS)
text:
11. Data model

Tabular data is the primary representation (CSV at v0.1; a database later). Each dataset has a documented schema and a README describing provenance and limitations.
Sample data is synthetic and labelled as such. It exists to exercise the machinery, never to stand in as market fact.
Zones: data/raw (immutable inputs), data/processed (derived, reproducible from raw + code), data/external (third-party), data/sample (synthetic, versioned).
Identity columns (segment_id, customer_id, evidence_id, claim_id) MUST be unique within their table.

title: Commercial Intelligence Specification (CIS)
text:
12. Validation model
Two layers, both specified in src/validation.py and enforced by tests/:

Structural validation — required columns present, identity columns unique, score columns within range [0, 100], no negative scores where the scale forbids them. Optionally enforced with pandera schemas / pydantic models where available.
Decision validation — a Recommendation is valid only if its provenance includes Evidence and a Validation step, and its confidence grade is consistent with the aggregate evidence strength.

Validation failures are errors, not warnings: they block promotion of a score or recommendation.

title: Commercial Intelligence Specification (CIS)
Commercial Intelligence Specification (CIS)
text:
14. Governance

CIS governs process; it does not dictate conclusions.
Changes to CIS itself are made through Architecture Decision Records (docs/decision-records/) and version bumps to this document.
Framework weight changes are versioned in the framework YAML and require a note of rationale.
No artifact may present an unvalidated number as validated. Reviewers enforce this.
The append-only rule applies to Decisions, Evidence, and ADRs.

title: Commercial Intelligence Specification (CIS)
text:
15. Computational architecture
data/ (CSV, synthetic sample)      frameworks/ (YAML weights)      cis/ (ontology, lifecycle)
        │                                   │                              │
        └───────────────┬───────────────────┴──────────────┬──────────────┘
                        ▼                                   ▼
                    src/  (scoring · validation · evidence · ontology · utils)
                        │
          ┌─────────────┼──────────────┐
          ▼             ▼              ▼
      tests/       dashboards/*.qmd   working-paper/*.qmd
   (pytest guards)  (Plotly, live)     (structured enquiry)
                        │
                        ▼
                  Quarto site (_site/)

src/ is the single source of computational truth. Dashboards and notebooks import from it; they do not re-implement scoring.
frameworks/ holds tunable parameters as data.
tests/ guards src/ and data integrity; nothing downstream is trusted if tests fail.

title: Commercial Intelligence Specification (CIS)
text:
16. Testing standard

Every scoring function MUST be tested for: output range [0, 100], missing-value safety, and monotonicity/sanity on at least one clear case.
Every validation function MUST be tested on both a passing and a failing input.
Evidence classification and aggregation MUST be tested against known inputs.
Sample datasets MUST have an integrity test (required columns present, ids unique, scores in range). The suite runs with pytest and must be green before results are trusted.

title: Commercial Intelligence Specification (CIS)
text:
17. Decision confidence
Every Recommendation carries a confidence grade:








Grade
Meaning
Typical basis




D — Hypothesis
Asserted, not yet evidenced.
Proposal claim only.


C — Indicative
Weak evidence, illustrative computation.
Sample/synthetic data, single source.


B — Substantiated
Moderate, corroborated evidence.
Multiple independent sources, validated method.


A — Validated
Strong evidence, method validated, monitored.
Convergent evidence + live metrics.



At v0.1 essentially everything is grade C or D. A recommendation’s grade MUST NOT exceed what its aggregate evidence strength (see §10) supports.

title: Commercial Intelligence Specification (CIS)
text:
18. Continuous learning
The Monitoring and Learning stages close the loop. Post-decision Metric observations are compared to the expectations the Recommendation stated. Divergence triggers revision: hypotheses are updated, evidence re-weighted, and, if needed, the Question re-opened. Learning is recorded, so the project has memory of what changed and why.

title: Commercial Intelligence Specification (CIS)
text:
19. Extension model
CIS is extended without breaking the governance contract by:

New frameworks — add a YAML file + scoring function + test; register its question.
New objects — extend ontology.yml and object-model.yml with a version bump; add an ADR.
New models — a statistical/ML model is a Model object; it must still pass through Validation and carry provenance. Advanced ML does not bypass the lifecycle.
New data sources — documented schema + README + integrity test.

Extensions MUST preserve: traceability, reproducibility, the hypothesis/fact separation, and the testing standard.

title: Commercial Intelligence Specification (CIS)
text:
20. Success criteria
CIS is succeeding when:

Every published recommendation can name its lifecycle provenance and confidence grade.
No unvalidated claim is presented as fact.
Re-running the repository reproduces every score and figure.
The test suite is green and covers all scoring/validation/evidence logic.
Evidence accumulates over time and visibly moves recommendations.
A newcomer can trace any conclusion back to its question and evidence unaided.

title: Commercial Intelligence Specification (CIS)
text:
21. Future work

Live CRM / RevOps data integration replacing synthetic samples.
pandera/pydantic schemas enforced in CI on real data.
Segmentation models (scikit-learn) as first-class Model objects.
Pricing and willingness-to-pay models with proper elasticity evidence.
A persistent decision store (database) with provenance queries.
Automated confidence-grade computation from the evidence register.
Knowledge-graph representation of the ontology (networkx → graph DB).


Document status: v0.1.0, Draft. This specification will itself evolve through ADRs and version bumps. Its authority is over process; conclusions remain earned through evidence.

title: Governance
text:
Governance › Overview


This section is how the lab governs itself. It holds the rules, the record of past choices, and the automated checks.


Process is enforced Read the standard


Governance answers “by what authority?” for everything else on the site. It has three pillars: the standard that defines how strategy is reasoned about, the decision records that capture why the project is built the way it is, and the testing standard that guards every calculation.



The standard


Commercial Intelligence Specification


The normative document: lifecycle, ontology, evidence model, confidence grades, and mandatory rules. This is the technical standard itself.

Read the CIS 



The record


Decision Records


Append-only Architecture Decision Records — why the lab uses this architecture and why CIS governs it.

Read the ADRs 



The guardrail


Testing Standard


What the automated test suite guarantees, what it covers, and how to run it.

See the tests 








Two views of the CIS, deliberately distinct. The version here in Governance is the standard itself. The Working Paper’s CIS chapter is a shorter discussion of how the standard is applied to this project. Read the standard for the rules; read the chapter for the application.

title: 9. Conclusion
text: Open · grades C/D No conclusions drawn by design

title: 9. Conclusion
section: What this paper has (and has not) established
text:
What this paper has (and has not) established


Has
A governed method (CIS), a structured set of falsifiable claims, illustrative frameworks that make those claims measurable, and a test suite guarding the machinery.


Has not
Validated any of the three decision questions. No segment, positioning, or GTM strategy is recommended yet.

title: 9. Conclusion
section: Current state of the three decision questions
text:
Current state of the three decision questions








Question
Current state
Gate to advance




Q1 — First segment
Illustrative ranking only (grade C)
Discovery / pilot evidence for top segments (Ch. 5)


Q2 — Positioning
Framing scored, untested on buyers (grade C)
Message-testing results (Ch. 4)


Q3 — Evidence threshold
Threshold defined (grade B gate)
Instrument the register; meet the gate (Ch. 7)

title: 9. Conclusion
text:
The central thesis
The proposal’s claim that the CRM is the foundation for AI readiness (H-CRM-AI) remains a hypothesis (grade D → C). The planned pilot (EV-010) is the pivotal next experiment.



What “done” looks like

A conclusion is warranted when the relevant claims reach the confidence grade their decisions require (≥ B for a GTM commitment), with reproducible evidence in the register and a green test suite. Until then, this chapter stays open — by design.

title: 9. Conclusion
text:
Immediate next steps


Next steps

Run the AI-readiness pilot (EV-010) and record outcomes (Ch. 6).
Run discovery calls for the top two segments (Ch. 5) and a message test for positioning (Ch. 4).
Re-run the dashboards and the evidence summary; let the numbers move.
Revisit this chapter only when evidence — not assertion — supports a verdict.

title: 7. Go-to-Market
text:
Decision 3 · Evidence threshold Gate at grade B




Decision question

What evidence is required before Sigmoid commits to a go-to-market (GTM) strategy — segment, positioning, motion, and pricing?



Hypothesis

A GTM commitment should be gated on reaching confidence grade B (substantiated) on three linked claims: the first-target segment (Ch. 5), the positioning (Ch. 4), and a defensible price/packaging. Committing earlier risks scaling an unvalidated thesis.
Falsifier: evidence that a fast, cheap commitment on weaker signal outperforms the gated approach (e.g. speed-to-market dominates in this category).




Current evidence

Proposal-level GTM and pricing assertions only — EV-003, EV-008 (grade D).
No willingness-to-pay evidence collected yet; the pricing framework is deliberately a placeholder.



Evidence needed

Segment: discovery / pilot conversion evidence (Ch. 5) at grade ≥ B.
Positioning: message-testing results (Ch. 4) at grade ≥ B.
Pricing: willingness-to-pay signals and competitor benchmarks (pricing_model.yml) — currently absent (EV-008, grade D).
Motion: whether the sale is founder-led, inbound, or partner-led for the chosen segment.



Analysis plan

Define the evidence threshold (grade B on the three linked claims) as the commit gate.
Track each claim’s evidence strength via the register and summarise_evidence_by_claim.
Only when all three clear the gate, draft a GTM recommendation with explicit confidence and basis.



Next steps

Instrument the evidence register so the three gate-claims’ strengths are visible at a glance.
Do not publish a GTM recommendation until the threshold is met.




Limitations

Grades depend on the quality / bias metadata being recorded honestly.
The three claims interact (positioning affects segment affects price); they may not clear the gate independently.

title: 5. Market Prioritisation
text:
Decision 1 · Market entry Confidence C · Indicative Companion: Market Prioritisation dashboard




Decision question

Which market segment should Sigmoid target first for the CRM?



Hypothesis

Segments with acute, funded pain and short sales cycles (initial candidates: audit and professional-services firms) will out-perform large-but-diffuse segments (SMEs) and slow-procurement public-sector segments (municipalities) as a first target.
Falsifier: discovery or pilot evidence showing a large-but-diffuse or public-sector segment converts faster and cheaper than professional-services firms.




Current evidence

Illustrative sub-scores for seven segments (synthetic) → priority ranking in the dashboard.
One discovery signal supporting audit firms — EV-005 (n = 1, grade C).
One signal cautioning against municipalities on procurement/budget — EV-006.
SME size-vs-budget tension — EV-009 (supports size, not budget).



Evidence needed

Per-segment: real pain acuteness, budget / willingness-to-pay, sales-cycle length, competitive intensity, and strategic fit.
Conversion signal from a handful of discovery calls or a pilot per candidate segment.



Analysis plan

Compute illustrative priority scores (done) to sequence which segments to investigate first.
Collect real sub-score evidence for the top 2–3 segments; replace synthetic inputs.
Re-run and test whether the ranking is stable under reasonable weight changes (sensitivity).



Next steps

Run discovery calls for the top two segments; register the evidence.
Only then consider a first-target recommendation, at the confidence its evidence supports.




Limitations

The ranking currently reflects assumptions encoded as sub-scores, not observations.
evidence_quality_score down-weights poorly-known segments, which can entrench what we already (think we) know — watch for this bias.

title: 3. Commercial Intelligence Specification
text:
Working Paper · applied discussion







This chapter vs. the standard. This is the applied discussion — how the CIS method is used in this project. For the normative standard itself (lifecycle, ontology, evidence model, mandatory rules), see Governance › Commercial Intelligence Specification.






Question

How do we ensure every commercial recommendation in this project is traceable, reproducible, falsifiable, and honest about its confidence?



Hypothesis

Treating commercial strategy as a computational decision system — with a fixed lifecycle, an evidence model, and an enforced testing standard — produces better, more revisable decisions than narrative strategy documents.



The method in brief



Lifecycle
Question → Hypothesis → Operational Definition → Evidence → Analysis → Model → Validation → Recommendation → Implementation → Monitoring → Learning.


Ontology & object model
Every claim, score, and decision is a typed object with identity and provenance (ontology.yml, object-model.yml).


Evidence model
Items are weighted by quality × bias adjustment; each claim gets a strength grade.


Confidence grades
D (hypothesis) → C (indicative) → B (substantiated) → A (validated). All logic under pytest.




Current evidence

The specification, ontology, object model, and lifecycle YAML exist and are internally consistent.
The test suite passes (see Ch. 8).



Evidence needed

Evidence that the method is actually followed (provenance present on recommendations).
A green test suite as a precondition for trusting any number.



Analysis plan
Audit each downstream chapter for lifecycle compliance: does each recommendation-in-waiting name its evidence and validation? Flag any number presented above its earned confidence grade.


Next steps
Apply the method to the three decision questions (Chs. 4–7).



Limitations

v0.1 enforces structure, not advanced modelling; sophistication is deferred (CIS §21).

title: 1. Introduction
text:
Confidence D · Framing Sets up Chapters 4–9




Guiding question

What must be true for Sigmoid Analytica’s CRM to become a defensible commercial and AI-readiness platform, and how will we know?



Hypothesis

The proposal’s thesis — that the CRM is the foundation for AI readiness and a wedge into a broader revenue-operations offering — is a coherent and plausible v0.1 hypothesis, but its key claims (segment, positioning, pricing, readiness) are currently asserted rather than evidenced.



The aim is to convert the proposal into a testable commercial enquiry: a set of questions about market, positioning, readiness, and go-to-market that can be answered with evidence and revised as that evidence changes.



Current evidence

The proposal itself, registered as source_type = proposal (grade D) in the evidence register.
A small number of synthetic / illustrative discovery signals, clearly labelled as such.



Evidence needed

The full set of falsifiable claims extracted from the proposal, each with an operational definition.
A baseline of which claims already have any supporting evidence, and of what quality.



Analysis plan

Read the paper’s structure (see below).
Extract claims into hypotheses (Chs. 4–7).
Score the illustrative frameworks to make claims measurable (dashboards).
Identify the highest-value evidence to collect next (Ch. 7).



Next steps

Proceed to Ch. 2 for the commercial context.
Treat every subsequent number as illustrative until Ch. 8’s validation is satisfied on real data.




Limitations

No real customer or market data at v0.1; all numbers are synthetic.
The proposal is a single source with high bias risk.








How to read this paper. Each chapter uses the same fixed structure. When a chapter states a hypothesis, read it as “this is what we currently believe and intend to test” — never as a finding.

import pandas as pd

OPTION_COLORS = {

    "Revenue Operations Platform": "#2f6bb0",
    "AI Readiness Platform": "#7a4fb0",
}
Analyst assessment of each option (synthetic, 0–10 per criterion)
# Rows = positioning options; columns = the Positioning Canvas criteria (each 0–10).

assessment = pd.DataFrame(
    [
        # option,                       clarity, diff, pain, investor, scale, evidence

    lambda row: weighted_score(row, weights), axis=1
)

Build summary metrics (computed, not hardcoded)
leader = ranked.iloc[0]
second = ranked.iloc[1]
margin = leader["positioning_score"] - second["positioning_score"]
weakest_evidence = assessment.sort_values("evidence_strength").iloc[0]

def metric(label, value, sub=""):
    sub_html = f'<div class="metric-sub">{sub}</div>' if sub else ""
    return (f'<div class="metric"><div class="metric-label">{label}</div>'
            f'<div class="metric-value">{value}</div>{sub_html}</div>')

HTML(
    '<div class="metric-row">'
    + metric("Options compared", len(ranked), "candidate positionings")
    + metric("Leading framing", leader["option"], f'score {leader["positioning_score"]:.1f}')
    + metric("Margin over #2", f'+{margin:.1f}', f'vs {second["option"]}')
    + metric("Weakest evidence", weakest_evidence["option"], "least-evidenced framing")
    + "</div>"
)

title: Positioning Dashboard
text:
Interpretation (provisional)

CRM Platform wins on clarity but is weak on differentiation — buyers get it instantly, yet see it as commoditised (cf. evidence EV-007).
Revenue Operations Platform balances differentiation, pain alignment, and scalability.
AI Readiness Platform scores highest on investor appeal but lowest on evidence strength — the most exciting story is the least evidenced today (cf. Hypothesis H-CRM-AI).







WarningThis is a framing tool, not a verdict



The scores are one analyst’s synthetic judgement. Positioning should be decided against customer evidence — message testing, win/loss analysis, discovery calls — not an internal scoring exercise. See Working Paper Ch. 4.

title: Frameworks
text:
How scoring works
The relevant src/scoring.py function reads a data row, rescales each sub-score, applies the direction (higher_better or lower_better), multiplies by the criterion weight, sums, and returns a value in [0, 100]. Missing values are handled safely (treated as the neutral midpoint) so a partial row never crashes the calculation.

title: Frameworks
text:
Adding a framework

Create frameworks/<name>.yml following the shape above (id, version, question, criteria with weight + direction, scale, status).
Ensure the weights sum to 1.0 (or document the normalisation rule).
If it introduces a new score, add a scoring function to src/scoring.py and a test to tests/test_scoring.py asserting the 0–100 range and missing-value safety.
Reference the framework from a dashboard and/or working-paper chapter.

title: First Evidence Sprint
text:
The fieldwork workflow


1Conduct interview


→2Enter raw data


→3Map to evidence


→4Review claims


→5Decide if status moves



1 · How to conduct an interview

Run the call with the discovery interview script (~40 min, 13 sections from respondent context to permission to follow up). Every question maps to a decision, a claim, and a CSV field.
Run the positioning message test inside the call to test the three framings (CRM · Revenue Operations · AI Readiness).
Score the interview the same day with the scoring guide — anchored 0–100 rubrics for every signal, plus quality and bias.







Interviewer discipline. Do not lead the witness; ask open questions first. Record what was actually said (verbatim in notes). One interview is a single data point — it never validates a claim. Leave a field blank if it was not covered; do not fabricate it.





2 · How to enter raw interview data
Add one row per interview to data/raw/discovery_interviews.csv, filling every covered field:
interview_id, date, company_segment, company_size, respondent_role, decision_linked, claim_linked,
pain_score, budget_signal, urgency_signal, current_tooling, crm_maturity, workflow_maturity,
ai_readiness_maturity, positioning_preference, willingness_to_pay_signal, sales_cycle_signal, notes,
evidence_quality_score, bias_risk, supports_or_refutes
All signal scores are 0–100 (see the scoring guide). Set decision_linked, claim_linked, and supports_or_refutes (supports / refutes / mixed / neutral) for the primary claim.


3 · How to map interview data into processed evidence
Using the evidence-capture step, add one row per claim the interview bears on to data/processed/discovery_evidence.csv:
evidence_id, interview_id, decision_id, claim_id, source_type, source_name, date_collected,
quality_score, bias_risk, supports_or_refutes, evidence_summary, confidence_impact, notes
Apply the conversions from the scoring guide:








Raw interview field (0–100)
Processed field

Set source_type = interview and confidence_impact = raises / lowers / none (direction only — never a grade). The Evidence Register is where these records land.


4 · How to run pytest and quarto render
source .venv/bin/activate
pytest            # validators check columns, grades (A–D), supports/refutes, and score ranges
quarto render     # the decision + Claims & Confidence pages recompute from the new records
Both must pass before you trust anything. A red suite is a hard stop.


5 · How to review results

Claims & Confidence — each tested claim now shows a real evidence count and a supports/refutes split instead of “none yet”.
Market Entry and Positioning — the claim cards reflect the new evidence.
Current Status — the top-level read of where the decisions stand.
Ask, per claim: did independent interviews converge, or does the signal conflict?

title: First Evidence Sprint
text:
What not to conclude yet







Do not declare a first-target segment or a positioning — a handful of interviews is grade-C signal at best.
Do not move any claim to grade A (validated); that needs a pilot with measured impact.
Do not treat a stated positioning preference as a won deal.
Do not let an enthusiastic-but-biased interview carry a claim — that is what bias_risk is for.
If a claim’s evidence_needed_for_B bar is genuinely met, change its grade deliberately and record why (a note or an ADR). Grade changes are decisions, not edits.




This sprint produces evidence, not verdicts. The lab stays at v0.1 hypothesis stage until the evidence earns otherwise.

section: Why the proposal is Hypothesis v0.1
text:
Why the proposal is Hypothesis v0.1

The source proposal is a persuasive argument made before the evidence is in. Treating it as truth would bake in untested assumptions.

So it enters the lab as Hypothesis v0.1: a set of falsifiable claims, each registered and scheduled for validation. Proposal-sourced claims carry low quality and high bias — they can raise a hypothesis, never validate one. See exactly which claims rest on the proposal under Evidence › Hypothesis v0.1.






What this means in practice. Every score, ranking, and figure on this site is computed from synthetic sample data and carries confidence grade C or D. Nothing is presented as a validated market fact. See the live picture on Current Status.

title: Product Brief
text:
The one-paragraph version
We took the Sigmoid Analytica CRM proposal and turned it from a slide-deck argument into a living decision system — a site where commercial strategy is broken into concrete decisions, scored with transparent, adjustable frameworks, and held to an explicit standard for what counts as evidence. It runs on real code with an automated test suite, so every number can be re-run and audited. Crucially, it is honest about what it does not yet know: nothing is presented as a validated fact.

title: Product Brief
section: Why it is built this way
text:
Why it is built this way
A proposal is an argument made before the evidence is in. If we treat it as truth, we bake in untested assumptions about market, positioning, and pricing. So the project is governed by one rule set — the Commercial Intelligence Specification (CIS) — that treats the proposal as Hypothesis v0.1 and forces every recommendation through a fixed lifecycle before it can claim authority. Every claim carries a confidence grade: D (asserted) → C (illustrative) → B (substantiated) → A (validated). Today everything is C or D, by design.

title: Hypothesis v0.1 · Proposal Source
text:
What would change a grade
A proposal claim moves off grade D only when independent evidence arrives — an interview, a survey, a market report, or (decisively) an experiment/pilot.
This discipline now runs through the evidence operating layer: the current hypotheses are tracked in data/sample/claim_register.csv (see Claims & Confidence), and each is raised toward grade B only when real discovery evidence — logged via the Evidence Register — meets its stated bar. The proposal seeds the hypotheses; it never validates them.






This separation of hypothesis source from evidence is the reason the whole lab exists. The full rationale is recorded in Decision Record ADR-0002.

title: AI Readiness Scorecard
text:
Dashboards › AI Readiness Scorecard


Supports the thesis H-CRM-AI — is the CRM the foundation for AI readiness? Context in Working Paper › AI Readiness.


Confidence D → C Synthetic data Related: Framework · Evidence Register
Imports and project path bootstrap
import sys, pathlib
_root = pathlib.Path.cwd()
while _root != _root.parent and not (_root / "src").is_dir():
    _root = _root.parent
sys.path.insert(0, str(_root))

import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
from IPython.display import HTML

from src.utils import load_sample
from src.scoring import calculate_ai_readiness_score, calculate_customer_fit_score

PLOTLY_TEMPLATE = "plotly_white"
Load customers and compute readiness + fit scores
customers = load_sample("customer_profiles.csv").copy()
customers["ai_readiness_score"] = customers.apply(calculate_ai_readiness_score, axis=1)
customers["customer_fit_score"] = customers.apply(calculate_customer_fit_score, axis=1)

def band(score):
    if score >= 66:
        return "High"
    if score >= 40:
        return "Medium"
    return "Low"

customers["readiness_band"] = customers["ai_readiness_score"].map(band)
ranked = customers.sort_values("ai_readiness_score", ascending=False).reset_index(drop=True)
Build summary metrics (computed, not hardcoded)
n = len(customers)
mean_readiness = customers["ai_readiness_score"].mean()
high_n = int((customers["readiness_band"] == "High").sum())
high_pct = 100 * high_n / n
top = ranked.iloc[0]

def metric(label, value, sub=""):
    sub_html = f'<div class="metric-sub">{sub}</div>' if sub else ""
    return (f'<div class="metric"><div class="metric-label">{label}</div>'
            f'<div class="metric-value">{value}</div>{sub_html}</div>')

HTML(
    '<div class="metric-row">'
    + metric("Customers scored", n, "synthetic profiles")
    + metric("Mean AI readiness", f"{mean_readiness:.1f}", "out of 100 (illustrative)")
    + metric("High-readiness share", f"{high_pct:.0f}%", f"{high_n} of {n} customers")
    + metric("Most ready", top["company_name"], f'score {top["ai_readiness_score"]:.1f}')
    + "</div>"
)

Interpretation

data_quality is the load-bearing input of this index because the proposal asserts AI value flows from the data/CRM foundation.

Readiness and fit are scored independently — treat any apparent relationship in the scatter as a question to test, not a finding.







ImportantThe claim under test



The heavy data_quality weight makes this index a restatement of Hypothesis H-CRM-AI (grade D → C). Validating it requires linking readiness scores to actual AI outcomes in a pilot — see Working Paper Ch. 6.

The index encodes the thesis rather than testing it — avoid circular reasoning when validating.

title: Market Prioritisation
text:
Interpretation

A high score means a segment scores well on the weighted blend of size, pain, budget, and fit, after penalising competition and long sales cycles.
evidence_quality_score deliberately discounts segments we know little about — a hedge against ranking on guesswork.
The lead over #2 is a scan of robustness: a small margin means the top choice is sensitive to weight changes and should not be over-read.







WarningLimitations




Sub-scores are assumptions encoded as numbers, not observations; the ranking inherits their bias.
Synthetic data only — nothing here is a decision. Promoting a segment to a recommendation requires evidence collection and validation (see Working Paper Ch. 5).









NoteNext step in the lifecycle



Collect real evidence for the top-ranked segments (interviews, pilots), register it in the evidence register, and re-run. The ranking should change as evidence — not assumptions — accumulates.

Commercial Health › The Check


This is the reference implementation of the Commercial Health Index Specification. Rate the statements; your score computes live in your browser.


Self-assessment · confidence grade C 7 dimensions · 21 statements

title: 2. Problem Context
text:
Confidence D · Framing




Question

What commercial situation is Sigmoid Analytica actually in, and which of the proposal’s framings of that situation are load-bearing?



Hypothesis

Sigmoid operates in a crowded CRM/analytics space where “CRM” alone is commoditised, but where a credible data-and-AI-readiness angle could differentiate — if customers feel the underlying pain and can act on it. The proposal treats this as given; here it is a hypothesis.




Current evidence

Proposal narrative (grade D) positioning CRM as the AI foundation — EV-001, EV-003.
One internal signal that prospects grasp “CRM” fastest but see it as commoditised — EV-007.
No independent market-structure evidence yet.



Evidence needed

Market structure: incumbents, substitutes, and how buyers currently frame their need.
Buyer pain: is CRM/data disorganisation an acute, funded problem for the target segments?
Sigmoid’s actual differentiators (capability, data assets, relationships), not aspirational ones.



Analysis plan

Map the competitive set and buyer language for each candidate segment.
Separate proposal assertions from observed signals in the evidence register.
Feed the pain / competition reads into the market-prioritisation framework to test sensitivity.



Next steps

Extract the specific commercial claims into hypotheses in Chs. 4–7.
Prioritise collecting buyer-pain evidence for the top segments (Ch. 5).




Limitations

The proposal is persuasive by design; its framing may bias which problems we look for.
Competitive and buyer evidence is currently absent.

title: 4. CRM Positioning
text:
Decision 2 · Positioning Confidence C · Indicative Companion: Positioning dashboard




Decision question

How should the product be positioned: as a CRM, a Revenue Operations Platform, or an AI Readiness Platform?



Hypothesis

A Revenue Operations Platform framing best balances clarity, differentiation, and pain alignment, while the AI Readiness Platform framing has the highest investor appeal but the weakest current evidence. This is a hypothesis to be tested against buyers, not a conclusion.
Falsifier: buyer message-testing in which “CRM” (or “AI Readiness”) consistently out-converts “RevOps” on comprehension and willingness to engage.




Current evidence

Proposal RevOps / AI-readiness narrative — EV-003 (grade D, mixed).
Internal note: “CRM” is understood fastest but seen as commoditised — EV-007.
Illustrative Positioning Canvas scores (synthetic) in the dashboard.



Evidence needed

Message-testing / A-B comprehension across the three framings, per segment.
Win/loss and discovery-call language: which frame do buyers repeat back?
Category evidence: which framing maps to a budget line buyers already hold?



Analysis plan

Score each option on the Positioning Canvas (done, illustratively) to frame the debate.
Run message tests; convert results into evidence_strength inputs and re-score.
Check sensitivity of the ranking to the evidence_strength and investor_appeal weights.



Next steps

Design and run a message test for the top two framings.
Register the results and re-run the dashboard; do not commit to a position at grade < B.




Limitations

Current option scores are one analyst’s synthetic judgement.
Investor appeal and customer pain can point in different directions; the weights encode a value choice that should be made explicit with stakeholders.

title: 6. AI Readiness
text:
Hypothesis H-CRM-AI Confidence D → C Companion: AI Readiness Scorecard




Decision question

Is the CRM genuinely the foundation for AI readiness — i.e. does CRM/data maturity predict the value a customer derives from AI?



Hypothesis (H-CRM-AI)

Customers with higher CRM/data and analytics maturity realise more value from AI features built on top of the CRM; therefore an AI Readiness Index anchored on data quality is a useful predictor of downstream AI value.
Falsifier: a pilot in which AI-derived value is uncorrelated with (or inversely related to) pre-pilot readiness scores.




Current evidence

Proposal assertion of the thesis — EV-001 (grade D).
Indirect secondary benchmark linking data maturity to AI value — EV-004 (grade C, medium bias).
A planned internal pilot to test the thesis — EV-010 (not yet run).
Illustrative readiness scores across synthetic customers (dashboard).



Evidence needed

A defined, measurable AI value outcome (e.g. time saved, conversion lift, forecast accuracy).
Paired data: pre-pilot readiness score and post-pilot realised value, across several customers.
Enough spread in readiness to detect a relationship.



Analysis plan

Finalise the operational definition of “AI value” (the outcome variable).
Run the pilot; record pre-pilot readiness and post-pilot value.
Test association (readiness vs value); check whether the data_quality weight is justified.
Recalibrate the index weights from evidence, not assumption.



Next steps

Execute EV-010 (the pilot). Until then, H-CRM-AI stays at grade D → C and must be labelled as such everywhere it appears.




Limitations

The index currently encodes the thesis (data quality heavily weighted) rather than testing it; circularity must be avoided when validating.
Small pilot n will limit statistical confidence.

title: Testing Standard
text:
Governance › Testing Standard


This page answers “can I trust the numbers?” It documents the checks that guard every calculation.


Green suite required Read the CIS standard
Under CIS §16, no score or recommendation is trusted unless the test suite is green. Tests guard the logic and structure of the project — a red suite is a hard stop, not a warning.

title: Testing Standard
text:
What the suite guarantees



Range safety


Every scoring function returns a value in 0–100, on full and partial rows.




Missing-value safety


Missing, null, or non-numeric inputs never crash a score — they fall back to a neutral midpoint.




Structural validation


Required columns present, identity columns unique, no negative scores where the scale forbids them.




Evidence integrity


Evidence weighting, strength classification, and per-claim aggregation match known inputs.

title: Testing Standard
text:
What each test file covers







Test file
Covers




tests/test_scoring.py
Market/customer/AI scores stay in 0–100; missing values are safe; weights behave (higher pain ↑, penalties ↓)


tests/test_validation.py
Score-range predicate; required-columns check; negative-score guard


tests/test_evidence.py
Evidence weight = quality × bias; strength classification bands; per-claim aggregation and sorting


tests/test_data_integrity.py
Sample data loads; required columns present; ids unique; computed scores in range

title: Testing Standard
text:
How to run it
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt
pytest
The suite currently holds 53 tests, all passing. Add a test whenever you add a scoring function or a framework — assert its 0–100 range and its missing-value safety at minimum.






Scope, honestly. Tests verify that the code and structure are correct. They do not verify that the synthetic inputs reflect reality — that is what the Evidence section and the eventual pilots are for.

title: Decision Records
text: 2 records Append-only

title: Decision Records
text:
Adding a record

Copy an existing ADR to ADR-XXXX-<slug>.md (next number).
Use the standard headings: Status, Context, Decision, Consequences, Alternatives considered.
Link it in the cards above.
To change a past decision, add a new ADR that supersedes it and update the old one’s Status.

See the README and the Governance section of the CIS for the full process.
Code

Context
The project’s inputs include a persuasive investor/operator proposal. Persuasive documents are written to argue a position before the evidence is in, and they mix genuine insight with untested assumptions about market, positioning, and pricing. We need an explicit rule for how such a document enters the project, and a governing model that keeps recommendations honest as evidence accumulates.

Decision

Adopt the Commercial Intelligence Specification (CIS) as the governance model for the project. CIS treats commercial strategy as a computational decision system with a fixed lifecycle (Question → … → Monitoring → Learning), a typed ontology and object model, an evidence model, a validation model, confidence grades (D–A), and a testing standard.
Treat the proposal PDF as Hypothesis v0.1 — a hypothesis source, not validated evidence. Its claims are extracted into falsifiable hypotheses and registered as evidence of source_type = proposal, capped at low quality and high bias risk. Under the evidence model such items can raise a hypothesis but cannot lift a claim above confidence grade C on their own.
CIS governs process, not conclusions. It dictates how claims are evidenced, validated, and graded — never what the answer must be. Where narrative and specification disagree about process, the specification wins; the narrative remains an input.

Consequences
Positive

No claim — however well-argued in the proposal — is presented as fact without an evidence trail.
Confidence is explicit and bounded by evidence strength, so readers can calibrate trust.
The strategy becomes revisable: as evidence arrives, grades and recommendations change in a documented way rather than by silent rewriting.
Reviewers have a concrete standard to enforce (“what stage, what evidence, what grade?”).

Negative / costs

More upfront discipline: every recommendation must name its provenance and grade.
It can feel slow to stakeholders expecting immediate answers from a compelling deck; this is the intended trade of speed for defensibility.

Alternatives considered

Treat the proposal as the project’s conclusions and build support around it. Rejected: this bakes in untested assumptions and defeats the purpose of an evidence-based lab.
Ad-hoc review with no formal model. Rejected: without a fixed lifecycle and confidence scale, “how sure are we?” gets answered inconsistently, and unvalidated numbers leak into decisions.

Notes
Changes to CIS itself are made through new ADRs and version bumps to cis/commercial-intelligence-specification.md. This record is append-only; supersede rather than edit.
Code

Signal scores (0–100)

pain_score — how acute and specific is the pain

0–20 No real pain; “we manage fine.”
21–40 Mild annoyance; generic grumbling, no cost attached.
41–60 Real, recurring pain named specifically, but tolerated.
61–80 Acute pain with a felt cost (time, rework, lost work) and frequency.
81–100 Severe, frequent, costed pain the respondent raised unprompted.
Claims: H-SEG-AUDIT-01, H-SEG-PROF-01 (D1).



budget_signal — is there money and a buyer

0–20 No budget, no owner, “we wouldn’t pay for this.”
21–40 Vague interest, no budget line identified.
41–60 A plausible owner named; budget “might exist.”
61–80 Clear budget owner + budget this year + prior spend on adjacent tools.
81–100 Active budget, named buyer, explicit intent to allocate.
Claim: H-SEG-AUDIT-02 (D1).



urgency_signal — is there a reason to act now

0–20 “Someday” problem; no timeline.
21–40 Would like to fix eventually.
41–60 On the roadmap, no compelling event.
61–80 This-quarter priority with a driver (growth, audit season, a failure).
81–100 Urgent, compelling event with a deadline.
Claim: H-SEG-AUDIT-01 (D1).



crm_maturity — sophistication and adoption of current CRM

0–20 Spreadsheets / email only; no CRM.
21–40 A CRM exists but is barely used.
41–60 CRM used by some of the team, inconsistently.
61–80 CRM well-adopted across the commercial team.
81–100 Mature, disciplined CRM practice with clean data.
Claims: H-POS-CRM-01, H-AI-READY-02 (D2).



workflow_maturity — how defined and standardised the commercial workflow is

0–20 Ad hoc; everything person-dependent.
21–40 Loose conventions, not written down.
41–60 Defined process, unevenly followed.
61–80 Standardised, documented, mostly followed.
81–100 Fully standardised and instrumented.
Claim: H-AI-READY-02 (D2).



ai_readiness_maturity — data structure + analytics practice

0–20 Data scattered; no analytics.
21–40 Some reports, mostly manual, data siloed.
41–60 Structured data in places; basic dashboards.
61–80 Clean, centralised data; regular analytics.
81–100 Strong data foundation, ready to operationalise AI.
Claims: H-AI-READY-01, H-AI-READY-02 (D2).



willingness_to_pay_signal — directional price reaction

0–20 Would not pay; expects free.
21–40 Would pay only a token amount.
41–60 Open to paying; no figure given.
61–80 Named a plausible monthly figure at/near expectations.
81–100 Named a figure at or above target, with conviction.
Claim: H-SEG-AUDIT-02 (D1). Keep directional — do not anchor the respondent.



sales_cycle_signal — ease/speed of buying (higher = shorter and simpler)

0–20 Very long cycle, many sign-offs, procurement-heavy.
21–40 Long cycle, committee decision.
41–60 Moderate; a couple of stakeholders.
61–80 Short; one or two decision-makers.
81–100 Fast; a single owner can decide.
Claim: H-GTM-GATE-01 (D1/D3). Note the inversion: high score = easy to buy.

Evidence-quality scores (0–100)

evidence_quality_score — how much weight this single source deserves
Judges rigour, directness, and recency of this interview. - 0–20 Hearsay, off-topic, or a very junior/uninformed respondent. - 21–40 Relevant but indirect; respondent generalising. - 41–60 Direct, first-hand account from a relevant role (typical ceiling for one discovery call). - 61–80 First-hand, decision-maker, specific and corroborated within the call. - 81–100 Rare for an interview — reserve for documented, verifiable evidence. - A one-off discovery call should seldom exceed 50.


bias_risk — how much this evidence may be distorted (higher = more biased = worse)
Consider: did we lead the witness? Is the respondent incentivised to please us? Selection bias in who we reached? Recall bias? - 0–20 Low risk: neutral questioning, disinterested respondent, representative selection. - 21–40 Some risk: minor leading, friendly respondent. - 41–66 Medium risk: warm intro, respondent wants to help us, some leading. - 67–85 High risk: clearly leading, respondent invested in our success. - 86–100 Severe: respondent is a friend/investor, or answers were coached.

Direction: supports_or_refutes
For each claim the interview touches, decide the direction (recorded in the interview row for the primary claim, and per-claim in the evidence records):

supports — the evidence makes the claim more likely true.
refutes — the evidence makes the claim less likely true.
mixed — genuinely cuts both ways.
neutral — touched on but uninformative.

Converting raw scores → processed evidence records
data/processed/discovery_evidence.csv uses the conventions the evidence model expects. Convert as follows when you create evidence records (see templates/evidence_capture_template.md):

The 0–100 signal scores (pain_score, budget_signal, …) stay in the interview row; they inform your narrative evidence_summary and confidence_impact but are not re-stored in the evidence record.

Reminder. These scores describe one source. Confidence grades in the registers change only by deliberate review when the evidence_needed_for_B bar is met by multiple independent, low-bias items — never automatically, and never from a single interview.
